summary: High‑energy New Year’s Eve 1971 show captured from a crisp FM broadcast; stellar performances (The Other One → Me & My Uncle → The Other One, NFA>GDTRFB>NFA) outweigh minor speed/DJ chatter issues.
review: This December 31 1971 Winterland broadcast delivers an electrifying set – the opening Dancin’ and the space‑filled Other One sequence are legendary, and the Not Fade Away → Goin’ Down The Road sandwich is a highlight.  The FM source is surprisingly clear with good balance, though some listeners note slight pitch inconsistencies and occasional DJ banter.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 30 reviews, the vast majority are 5‑star praises of the musical performance; recording issues (speed/chatter) are noted but do not detract from overall enjoyment.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Clear FM broadcast mix with good instrument separation; occasional DJ chatter and reported slight speed/pitch variations ("chipmunk" effect) on some tracks, but overall listenable.

standout_songs: The Other One > Me & My Uncle > The Other One
Not Fade Away > Goin' Down the Road > Not Fade Away
show_quality.setlist_flow: A high‑octane New Year's set that builds from a punchy Dancin’ opener through a jam‑heavy first half, then launches into an epic second set with seamless transitions (Space → Black Peter, NFA sandwich) and a strong finish.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Praised for soaring leads on Tennessee Jed, Loser, Dancin’, and the fluid solo in The Other One; occasional lyric slip‑ups noted but overall stellar.
band_member_comments.Phil: Recognized for crisp bass work early in the show and driving lines on China Cat; some reviewers miss his piano/keyboards in the mix.
band_member_comments.Bob: Bobby’s charismatic vocals (The Same Thing, rap intros) and guitar chops highlighted; praised for humor and energy throughout.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith’s new piano adds depth and space‑filled fills; a few comments about it being buried in the FM mix but generally appreciated for enhancing the new 1971 sound.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill (drummer) lauded for furious, rolling drumming on NFA sandwich, Truckin’, and overall high energy; described as “killing” the kit.
